scenario: Driftwood Marketplace ingests 4500 messages per hour into Camunda 8.8 from its vendor pricing pipeline. Each update message should automatically create one instance of the price-sync process, without any external service explicitly calling the CreateProcessInstance command from a client.
question: Which BPMN start event creates exactly one new process instance for each incoming pricing update message published to the cluster?

options.text: A message start event whose message name matches the published message name; for each new message a new process instance is created by the engine on receipt.
A signal start event whose signal name matches the broadcast signal; all subscribed processes share a single instance created once per broadcast across the cluster.
A timer start event configured with a 1-second interval; the engine creates one instance per tick and the worker correlates each pricing update message inside.
A conditional start event referencing a process variable mutated by an inbound connector; the engine creates one instance per change event detected on the variable.

a.text: Correct. A process with a message start event can be started by publishing a message whose name matches the start event name, and for each new message a new process instance is created on the cluster.

b.text: Incorrect. Signals broadcast to subscribers and do not implement per-message one-instance semantics the way a message start event does for each published message name.

c.text: Incorrect. A timer start event creates instances when the timer triggers, not per inbound message; correlation through a timer does not match the per-message semantics required here.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented implicit-creation events are message and timer start events; a conditional start event is not the documented mechanism for per-message instance creation.

explanation: A message start event is the documented mechanism for implicit per-message instance creation in Camunda 8: each new message whose name matches the start event creates a new process instance.
